title: Linux Kernel Development
subtitle: 3rd Edition
author: Robert Love
authorBio: Open-source programmer, speaker, and author with over 15 years of Linux involvement. Senior software engineer at Google, where he was a member of the team that developed the Android mobile platform's kernel. Former Chief Architect, Linux Desktop at Novell. Kernel contributions span the preemptive kernel, process scheduler, kernel events layer, inotify, and VM enhancements. Contributing editor for Linux Journal and author of Linux System Programming and Linux in a Nutshell.
themes: The Process Model: task_struct, clone(), kernel threads
Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS): vruntime, red-black tree, niceness
System Call Interface: sys_call_table, ioctl, syscall entry/exit
Kernel Data Structures: list_head, hlist, kfifo, rb_tree, IDR
Interrupt Control: top-half vs bottom-half, softirqs, tasklets, workqueues
Kernel Synchronization: atomic_t, spinlocks, mutexes, RCU
Memory Management: struct page, slab allocator, kmalloc, vmalloc, GFP flags
Virtual Filesystem: superblock, inode, dentry, file objects
ext2/ext3/ext4: indirect blocks, journaling, extent trees, delayed allocation
Networking in the Kernel: sk_buff, socket layer, NAPI, netfilter
Kernel Debugging: printk, oops, kgdb, ftrace, KASAN
Contributing to the Linux kernel: patch submission, coding style, community norms
excerpt: Linux Kernel Development details the design and implementation of the Linux kernel, presenting the content in a manner that is beneficial to those writing and developing kernel code, as well as to programmers seeking to better understand the operating system and become more efficient and productive in their coding. Written by a core kernel contributor, it covers process management, CFS scheduling, system calls, kernel data structures, interrupt handling, kernel synchronization, memory management, the VFS, filesystems, networking, and debugging — grounded in real kernel source code from the Linux 2.6.3x kernel series.
keyIdeas: task_struct is the kernel's representation of every process and thread; understanding it is a map to reading any kernel code
CFS models an ideal multitasking CPU with vruntime and a red-black tree, achieving fairness without fixed time slices
Linux threads are processes sharing an address space; clone() flags express this sharing declaratively (CLONE_VM, CLONE_FS, CLONE_THREAD, etc.)
Bottom halves (softirqs, tasklets, workqueues) defer heavy processing out of interrupt context to avoid starving the system
Kernel synchronization follows a layered primitive structure: atomics → spinlocks → rwlocks → seqlocks → mutexes → RCU, each with specific trade-offs
kmalloc() returns physically contiguous memory suitable for DMA; vmalloc() returns virtually contiguous but physically noncontiguous memory
The VFS abstracts access through four objects: superblock, inode, dentry, file — each with a precise lifecycle and role
ext4 replaced indirect block pointers with extent trees, dramatically reducing metadata overhead through contiguous physical block ranges
The proc filesystem and seq_file interface provide structured kernel-to-userspace communication without syscalls
Contributing patches to mainline Linux requires coding style compliance, Signed-off-by traceability, and familiarity with the kernel community workflow
keyTakeaways: do_fork() is the shared backend of fork(), vfork(), and clone() — the clone() flags are what actually differentiate thread creation
A kernel thread has mm=NULL and borrows its address space via active_mm, avoiding a full address-space switch
CFS has no fixed quantum; vruntime grows inversely proportional to nice-based task weight; leftmost red-black tree node is always next to run
System calls cross via syscall instruction; the syscall number indexes sys_call_table; glibc translates negative error returns into errno
Softirqs run immediately after hard interrupt; tasklets defer further without blocking context; workqueues run deferrable work in process context where sleeping is safe
Spinlocks cannot sleep; mutexes can. Choosing between them means choosing between atomic and process context
GFP_KERNEL may sleep and trigger reclaim; GFP_ATOMIC never sleeps — using the wrong flag in the wrong context causes deadlocks detectable by lockdep
container_of(ptr, type, member) recovers the parent struct from an embedded list_head — the most-used kernel C idiom
copy_to_user and copy_from_user handle page faults and permission checks that memcpy silently ignores; never memcpy to/from user space
jiffies monotonically wraps every ~50 days at HZ=1000; use time_after(), time_before() for comparisons; use ktime_t / hrtimer for high resolution
whoShouldRead: Systems programmers who want to understand Linux kernel internals to write more efficient or safer code
Developers learning to write kernel modules, character drivers, or block device drivers
Embedded engineers porting or optimizing Linux for ARM, MIPS, or other target boards
University students in operating systems courses who need a practical kernel reference alongside theory
Performance engineers diagnosing scheduling, memory, or I/O latency on Linux production systems
Developers preparing to contribute patches to mainline Linux
whoShouldSkip: Absolute beginners who have never used Linux from the command line (start with Linux in a Nutshell first)
Developers exclusively in userspace with no interest in kernel internals
Readers looking for coverage of modern 4.x/5.x/6.x kernel subsytems — eBPF, cgroups v2, io_uring, Btrfs — (this book covers 2.6.x)
Those seeking a line-by-line source code commentary (this book deliberately focuses on subsystems and interfaces)
coreThemes: The kernel as a set of well-defined subsystems with clean interfaces — mastering one opens understanding of all
Practical over theoretical: every concept grounded in real kernel source and daily development experience
Community participation: contributing patches, coding style, Signed-off-by, and maintainers
Performance and correctness: why each design choice was made, not just what it does
Portability: writing kernel code that works across architectures (x86, ARM, PowerPC, etc.)
